graph_vector_fulltext.message:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to say how cumulative penalties must be assessed across *different* categories of obligations (prohibited practices vs high‑risk vs transparency vs GPAI) for the same deployment, or whether multiple separate fines are allowed/required in that scenario.

What the context does say is:
- Member States must lay down “effective, proportionate and dissuasive” penalties and “respect the ne bis in idem principle,” and when assessing the *amount of the fines* they must consider, case-by-case, the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and its consequences, and the size of the provider (including if it’s an SME/start-up).
- The Regulation also mentions “upper limits” for administrative fines for certain specific infringements, and that compliance (including for providers of general-purpose AI models) is enforceable “inter alia, by means of fines.”

From this excerpt alone, the “ne bis in idem” reference signals a constraint against double punishment for the *same* infringement, but it doesn’t specify how to treat multiple legally distinct infringements that arise from the same AI system/deployment. If you can share the specific Articles/recitals on penalties (or the relevant page text) covering prohibited practices, high-risk, transparency, and GPAI fine levels, I can reconcile them and answer whether the text resolves (or leaves) ambiguity on multiple fines versus a single aggregated fine.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: The Regulation states that penalties should be 'effective, proportionate and dissuasive' and must respect the 'ne bis in idem principle.' However, a single AI system deployment could simultaneously violate prohibited practice provisions, high-risk requirements, transparency obligations, and GPAI model obligations. How should cumulative penalties be assessed, and does the ne bis in idem reference create ambiguity about whether multiple fines can be imposed for violations arising from the same AI system?
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
I don’t have enough information in the provided context to say how cumulative penalties must be assessed across *different* categories of obligations (prohibited practices vs high‑risk vs transparency vs GPAI) for the same deployment, or whether multiple separate fines are allowed/required in that scenario.

What the context does say is:
- Member States must lay down “effective, proportionate and dissuasive” penalties and “respect the ne bis in idem principle,” and when assessing the *amount of the fines* they must consider, case-by-case, the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and its consequences, and the size of the provider (including if it’s an SME/start-up).
- The Regulation also mentions “upper limits” for administrative fines for certain specific infringements, and that compliance (including for providers of general-purpose AI models) is enforceable “inter alia, by means of fines.”

From this excerpt alone, the “ne bis in idem” reference signals a constraint against double punishment for the *same* infringement, but it doesn’t specify how to treat multiple legally distinct infringements that arise from the same AI system/deployment. If you can share the specific Articles/recitals on penalties (or the relevant page text) covering prohibited practices, high-risk, transparency, and GPAI fine levels, I can reconcile them and answer whether the text resolves (or leaves) ambiguity on multiple fines versus a single aggregated fine.

Content: Text Content:
 respect the ne bis in idem principle. In order to strengthen and harmonise administrative  penalties for infringement of this Regulation, the upper limits for setting the administrative fines for certain specific  infringements should be laid down. When assessing the amount of the fines, Member States should, in each  individual case, take into account all relevant circumstances of the specific situation, with due regard in particular to  the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and of its consequences and to the size of the provider
----
 models imposed under this Regulation should  be enforceable, inter alia, by means of fines. To that end, appropriate levels of fines should also be laid down for  infringement of those obligations, including the failure to comply with measures requested by the Commission in  accordance with this Regulation, subject to appropriate limitation periods in accordance with the principle of  proportionality. All decisions taken by the Commission under this Regulation are subject to review by the Court of  Justice of the European Union in accordance with the
----
 interests, the integrity  of criminal and administrative proceedings, and the integrity of classified information. (168) Compliance with this Regulation should be enforceable by means of the imposition of penalties and other  enforcement measures. Member States should take all necessary measures to ensure that the provisions of this  Regulation are implemented, including by laying down effective, proportionate and dissuasive penalties for their  infringement, and to respect the ne bis in idem principle. In order to strengthen and harmonise administrative  penalties for
----
 the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and of its consequences and to the size of the provider, in  particular if the provider is an SME, including a start-up. The European Data Protection Supervisor should have the  power to impose fines on Union institutions, agencies and bodies falling within the scope of this Regulation. (169) Compliance with the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models imposed under this Regulation should  be enforceable, inter alia, by means of fines.
----

chunks.text: respect the ne bis in idem principle. In order to strengthen and harmonise administrative penalties for infringement of this Regulation, the upper limits for setting the administrative fines for certain specific infringements should be laid down. When assessing the amount of the fines, Member States should, in each individual case, take into account all relevant circumstances of the specific situation, with due regard in particular to the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and of its consequences and to the size of the provider

models imposed under this Regulation should be enforceable, inter alia, by means of fines. To that end, appropriate levels of fines should also be laid down for infringement of those obligations, including the failure to comply with measures requested by the Commission in accordance with this Regulation, subject to appropriate limitation periods in accordance with the principle of proportionality. All decisions taken by the Commission under this Regulation are subject to review by the Court of Justice of the European Union in accordance with the

interests, the integrity of criminal and administrative proceedings, and the integrity of classified information. (168) Compliance with this Regulation should be enforceable by means of the imposition of penalties and other enforcement measures. Member States should take all necessary measures to ensure that the provisions of this Regulation are implemented, including by laying down effective, proportionate and dissuasive penalties for their infringement, and to respect the ne bis in idem principle. In order to strengthen and harmonise administrative penalties for

the nature, gravity and duration of the infringement and of its consequences and to the size of the provider, in particular if the provider is an SME, including a start-up. The European Data Protection Supervisor should have the power to impose fines on Union institutions, agencies and bodies falling within the scope of this Regulation. (169) Compliance with the obligations on providers of general-purpose AI models imposed under this Regulation should be enforceable, inter alia, by means of fines.
